questions.question: Although the government has increased its spending on renewable energy, _____.

options.A: it still relies heavily on fossil fuels to meet its electricity demands.
options.B: the cost of solar panels has significantly dropped in recent years.
options.C: wind power is now the most efficient source of energy available.
options.D: most citizens are unaware of the importance of recycling.
options.E: international agreements require countries to reduce carbon emissions.

questions.question: Unless stricter regulations are enforced on industrial waste disposal, _____.

options.A: the health of the local population will continue to be at risk.
options.B: many companies have already started using eco-friendly materials.
options.C: water pollution in the river has decreased over the last decade.
options.D: local authorities are satisfied with the current environmental status.
options.E: new technologies could help filter toxins more effectively.

questions.question: The success of the new educational program depends on _____.

options.A: how well the teachers are trained to implement the curriculum.
options.B: the fact that students are not interested in extracurricular activities.
options.C: whether the school building was renovated three years ago.
options.D: the number of books available in the city public library.
options.E: the historical background of the region where the school is located.

questions.question: Just as some species have adapted to the harsh conditions of the desert, _____.

options.A: others have evolved to thrive in the freezing temperatures of the Arctic.
options.B: the desert landscape is often characterized by sand dunes and rocks.
options.C: human interference has caused many habitats to be destroyed.
options.D: global warming is a major threat to biodiversity worldwide.
options.E: biologists are studying the genetic makeup of rare plants.

questions.question: Since the invention of the printing press in the 15th century, _____.

options.A: information has been disseminated much more rapidly than ever before.
options.B: books were only available to the wealthy and the clergy.
options.C: many people had no access to formal education or literacy.
options.D: the internet has transformed the way we communicate today.
options.E: manuscripts were laboriously copied by hand by monks.

questions.question: While some economists argue that high taxes hinder economic growth, _____.

options.A: others maintain that they are necessary to fund essential public services.
options.B: the unemployment rate has remained stable despite the recent crisis.
options.C: stock markets have reacted positively to the new trade agreement.
options.D: inflation is often caused by an excess supply of money in the economy.
options.E: governments should focus on reducing their national debt.

questions.question: Due to the fact that the experimental data was inconsistent, _____.

options.A: the researchers decided to repeat the trial under stricter conditions.
options.B: the results were published in a highly prestigious medical journal.
options.C: everybody was convinced that the hypothesis was correct.
options.D: the funding for the project was doubled by the university.
options.E: the team celebrated their breakthrough in the laboratory.

questions.question: No matter how hard the negotiators tried to reach a compromise, _____.

options.A: the two parties could not agree on the terms of the treaty.
options.B: the meeting was held in a neutral location in Switzerland.
options.C: they were able to sign the agreement by the end of the week.
options.D: everyone was hopeful that a resolution would be found soon.
options.E: the mediator suggested a different approach to the problem.

questions.question: As soon as the results of the clinical trial are released, _____.

options.A: the pharmaceutical company will apply for regulatory approval.
options.B: the patients had been monitored for a period of six months.
options.C: doctors are usually skeptical of new medical treatments.
options.D: the laboratory was equipped with the latest technology.
options.E: several side effects were reported during the initial phase.

questions.question: In spite of the significant advancements in artificial intelligence, _____.

options.A: it is still unable to replicate the depth of human emotional intelligence.
options.B: it has become an integral part of our daily lives and industries.
options.C: machines can process data much faster than the human brain.
options.D: many jobs are being automated to increase efficiency and profit.
options.E: scientists are working on creating even more complex algorithms.

questions.question: Many historians argue that the decline of the Roman Empire was caused by a combination of internal strife and external invasions.

options.A: Pek çok tarihçi, Roma İmparatorluğu'nun çöküşünün iç çatışmalar ve dış istilaların birleşimiyle gerçekleştiğini savunmaktadır.
options.B: Çoğu tarihçiye göre Roma İmparatorluğu'nun yıkılmasının asıl nedeni iç karışıklıklar ve dış saldırılardır.
options.C: Bazı tarihçiler, Roma İmparatorluğu'nun hem iç hem de dış nedenlerle zayıfladığını ileri sürmektedir.
options.D: Tarihçilerin çoğu, iç savaşların ve dış göçlerin Roma'nın sonunu getirdiğini iddia eder.
options.E: Roma İmparatorluğu'nun gerilemesi, tarihçiler tarafından iç huzursuzluk ve dış baskılara bağlanmaktadır.

questions.question: Unless nations cooperate on a global scale, it will be impossible to effectively combat the threat posed by climate change.

options.A: Uluslar küresel ölçekte iş birliği yapmadıkça, iklim değişikliğinin oluşturduğu tehditle etkili bir şekilde mücadele etmek imkansız olacaktır.
options.B: Küresel bir iş birliği olmazsa, iklim değişikliği tehdidiyle mücadele etmek her ülke için zorlaşacaktır.
options.C: İklim değişikliği tehdidine karşı ancak uluslar arası iş birliği ile başarıya ulaşılabilir.
options.D: Ulusların küresel düzeyde birlikte çalışması, iklim değişikliği ile mücadelenin tek yoludur.
options.E: Dünya genelinde bir dayanışma sağlanırsa, iklim değişikliğinin etkileri tamamen ortadan kaldırılabilir.

questions.question: The invention of the internet has not only revolutionized communication but also transformed the global economy.

options.A: İnternetin icadı sadece iletişimde devrim yaratmakla kalmamış, aynı zamanda küresel ekonomiyi de dönüştürmüştür.
options.B: İnternet icat edildiğinde hem iletişim yöntemleri hem de dünya ekonomisi kökten değişti.
options.C: İletişimi devrimsel bir şekilde değiştiren internet, küresel ekonomiye de büyük katkı sağlamıştır.
options.D: İnternetin keşfi iletişimde bir devrim başlattığı gibi küresel piyasaları da etkilemiştir.
options.E: Küresel ekonomi ve iletişim, internetin icadı sayesinde tamamen yeni bir hal almıştır.

questions.question: Dünya çapında milyonlarca insan temiz suya erişimden yoksundur ve bu durum ciddi sağlık sorunlarına yol açmaktadır.

options.A: Millions of people worldwide lack access to clean water, and this situation leads to serious health problems.
options.B: Access to clean water is a problem for millions, causing many diseases around the world.
options.C: Serious health issues are common because millions of people do not have clean water.
options.D: Since millions lack clean water globally, health problems are becoming increasingly severe.
options.E: Lack of clean water worldwide is the main cause of health issues for millions of people.

questions.question: Bazı araştırmacılar, genetik faktörlerin bir bireyin zekasını belirlemede çevresel faktörlerden daha önemli olduğunu düşünmektedir.

options.A: Some researchers believe that genetic factors are more important than environmental factors in determining an individual's intelligence.
options.B: According to some researchers, environment is less significant than genetics in terms of intelligence.
options.C: Intelligence is determined by both genetics and environment, but some say genetics is more vital.
options.D: Research shows that genetic factors play a bigger role than environment in intelligence.
options.E: A number of scientists suggest that an individual's intelligence depends mostly on genetic factors.

questions.question: Son yıllarda yapılan kazılar, bu bölgenin bir zamanlar çok gelişmiş bir medeniyete ev sahipliği yaptığını kanıtlamıştır.

options.A: Excavations carried out in recent years have proved that this region once hosted a very advanced civilization.
options.B: Recent excavations show that an advanced civilization used to live in this specific area.
options.C: It has been proven by recent excavations that a highly developed society once existed here.
options.D: The region was home to an advanced civilization, as evidenced by the excavations of late.
options.E: Excavations in the last few years prove the existence of an old and developed civilization in the area.

questions.question: Passage 1: The transition from the Holocene to the Anthropocene marks a significant epochal shift characterized by human dominance over global environmental systems. While the Holocene provided a stable climate that allowed for the rise of agriculture and complex civilizations, the Anthropocene is defined by anthropogenic forcing—primarily through carbon emissions, deforestation, and plastic accumulation—that threatens to breach planetary boundaries. Critics argue whether this epoch should be officially recognized by the International Commission on Stratigraphy, citing the difficulty of pinpointing a precise start date, though many suggest the mid-20th century 'Great Acceleration' as the definitive marker. According to the passage, what distinguishes the Anthropocene from the Holocene?

options.A: The complete absence of natural climate fluctuations during the Holocene.
options.B: The shift from natural environmental stability to human-induced global changes.
options.C: The discovery of plastic as a primary building material in early civilizations.
options.D: A reduction in agricultural productivity due to technological advancements.
options.E: The official recognition of the epoch by the International Commission on Stratigraphy.

questions.question: Passage 1: The transition from the Holocene to the Anthropocene marks a significant epochal shift... Many suggest the mid-20th century 'Great Acceleration' as the definitive marker. It can be inferred from the passage that the 'Great Acceleration' refers to:

options.A: The rapid expansion of the International Commission on Stratigraphy.
options.B: A period of decreased human impact on the environment.
options.C: A significant surge in human activity and its environmental consequences.
options.D: The speed at which the Holocene gave way to the Anthropocene.
options.E: The technological race between nations during the mid-20th century.

questions.question: Passage 1: The transition from the Holocene to the Anthropocene marks a significant epochal shift... Why do some critics hesitate to officially recognize the Anthropocene?

options.A: They believe human impact is too insignificant to warrant a new epoch.
options.B: They disagree with the mid-20th century as the starting point.
options.C: They find it challenging to establish a specific chronological beginning.
options.D: They argue that plastic accumulation is a natural phenomenon.
options.E: They believe the Holocene has not yet concluded its natural cycle.

questions.question: Passage 1: The transition from the Holocene to the Anthropocene marks a significant epochal shift... What is the author's primary purpose in this passage?

options.A: To advocate for stricter regulations on carbon emissions.
options.B: To describe the characteristics and debate surrounding a new geological epoch.
options.C: To compare the agricultural techniques of the Holocene and Anthropocene.
options.D: To criticize the International Commission on Stratigraphy for its indecision.
options.E: To prove that the Great Acceleration was caused by plastic waste.

questions.question: Passage 2: The 'Sturm und Drang' movement in late 18th-century German literature represented a reaction against the perceived constraints of Enlightenment rationalism. Writers such as Goethe and Schiller championed individual subjectivity and the 'genius' over universal rules of aesthetics. This period emphasized intense emotion and the sublime power of nature, often portraying protagonists driven by overwhelming passion rather than cool-headed logic. Although brief, the movement laid the essential groundwork for the subsequent Romantic era, which would further explore the complexities of the human psyche. What was the primary motivation behind the Sturm und Drang movement?

options.A: To reinforce the principles of Enlightenment rationalism.
options.B: To establish universal rules for German aesthetic beauty.
options.C: To rebel against the limitations of purely logical thought.
options.D: To discourage the exploration of the human psyche in literature.
options.E: To support the political structures of late 18th-century Germany.

questions.question: Passage 2: The 'Sturm und Drang' movement... How does the passage characterize the relationship between Sturm und Drang and Romanticism?

options.A: Romanticism was a direct rejection of Sturm und Drang ideals.
options.B: Sturm und Drang served as a precursor to the Romantic era.
options.C: Both movements occurred simultaneously and shared the same founders.
options.D: Sturm und Drang focused on logic while Romanticism focused on emotion.
options.E: The Romantic era was less concerned with the human psyche than Sturm und Drang.

questions.question: Passage 2: The 'Sturm und Drang' movement... According to the passage, protagonists in Sturm und Drang literature are typically:

options.A: Governed by societal norms and rational thinking.
options.B: Indifferent to the power of nature and the sublime.
options.C: Motivated by intense, often uncontrollable emotions.
options.D: Critics of the concept of the 'individual genius'.
options.E: Primarily focused on scientific exploration and Enlightenment.

questions.question: Passage 2: The 'Sturm und Drang' movement... The word 'championed' in the passage is closest in meaning to:

options.A: Defeated
options.B: Advocated
options.C: Ignored
options.D: Regulated
options.E: Simulated

questions.question: Passage 3: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. This phenomenon challenges the traditional view that the adult brain is a static organ. Research indicates that neuroplasticity is driven by various factors, including environmental stimuli, learning, and even physical injury. When one part of the brain is damaged, other areas can sometimes compensate for the lost function by rerouting signals. However, this adaptability also means the brain can be negatively influenced by chronic stress or addictive behaviors, which 'wire' the brain in maladaptive ways. What is the central message regarding the adult brain in the passage?

options.A: It is a fixed structure that cannot change after childhood.
options.B: It possesses an inherent ability to adapt and rewire itself.
options.C: It is only capable of change following a traumatic physical injury.
options.D: It is immune to the negative effects of environmental stimuli.
options.E: It primarily relies on a single area for all cognitive functions.

questions.question: Passage 3: Neuroplasticity... According to the passage, how does the brain respond to damage in a specific area?

options.A: It immediately ceases all neural activity to prevent further harm.
options.B: Other regions may take over the functions of the damaged part.
options.C: It loses the ability to form any new neural connections.
options.D: It reverts to a pre-adult state of development.
options.E: It becomes permanently static and incapable of rerouting signals.

questions.question: Passage 3: Neuroplasticity... The passage suggests that neuroplasticity is a 'double-edged sword' because:

options.A: It only works for learning but fails during physical recovery.
options.B: It can lead to both beneficial learning and harmful habits.
options.C: It requires a balance between environmental and genetic factors.
options.D: The brain can only rewire itself once in a lifetime.
options.E: It is a theory that has been recently debunked by neuroscientists.

questions.question: Passage 3: Neuroplasticity... Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a driver of neuroplasticity?

options.A: Learning
options.B: Environmental stimuli
options.C: Genetic predestination
options.D: Physical injury
options.E: Chronic stress

questions.question: Passage 4: The Silk Road was not a single thoroughfare but a network of trade routes connecting East Asia with the Mediterranean. Beyond the exchange of silk, spices, and precious metals, it served as a vital conduit for cultural, religious, and technological diffusion. Buddhism spread from India to China along these routes, while papermaking and gunpowder moved westward. However, the Silk Road was also a vector for disease; the Black Death is believed to have traveled via these trade networks to Europe in the 14th century. The decline of the Silk Road was eventually precipitated by the rise of maritime trade, which offered safer and faster alternatives. What does the passage suggest about the nature of the Silk Road?

options.A: It was a primary road used exclusively for the transport of silk.
options.B: It facilitated the exchange of both goods and ideological concepts.
options.C: It was established primarily to spread the Black Death across Europe.
options.D: It remained the dominant trade method until the 20th century.
options.E: It was restricted to the borders of the Mediterranean region.

questions.question: Passage 4: The Silk Road... According to the text, which technologies moved from East to West?

options.A: Silk and spices
options.B: Buddhism and religion
options.C: Papermaking and gunpowder
options.D: Maritime navigation and shipbuilding
options.E: Gold and precious metals

questions.question: Passage 4: The Silk Road... What led to the eventual decrease in the importance of the Silk Road?

options.A: The complete eradication of silk production in China.
options.B: The outbreak of the Black Death in the 14th century.
options.C: The development of more efficient sea-based trade routes.
options.D: The refusal of Mediterranean countries to trade with East Asia.
options.E: The religious conflicts that prevented safe passage for merchants.

questions.question: Passage 4: The Silk Road... The author mentions the Black Death to illustrate that:

options.A: Trade routes had negative as well as positive consequences.
options.B: The Silk Road was poorly managed by historical authorities.
options.C: Europe was the most vulnerable region during the 14th century.
options.D: The spread of religion was more important than the spread of disease.
options.E: Maritime trade was safer because it didn't carry diseases.

questions.question: Passage 5: Game Theory, a branch of mathematics developed by John von Neumann and Oskar Morgenstern, analyzes strategic interactions where the outcome for one participant depends on the actions of others. While initially applied to economics, it has become essential in political science, biology, and philosophy. One of its most famous models, the 'Prisoner's Dilemma,' demonstrates why two rational individuals might not cooperate, even if it appears in their best interest to do so. The theory assumes participants are rational actors seeking to maximize their utility, though behavioral economists often critique this for ignoring human psychology. What is a fundamental premise of Game Theory as described in the passage?

options.A: Participants always cooperate to achieve the best collective result.
options.B: Outcomes are independent of the choices made by other participants.
options.C: Individuals act rationally to achieve the highest possible benefit for themselves.
options.D: It is only applicable to monetary transactions in economics.
options.E: Human psychology is the primary driver of strategic decision-making.

questions.question: Passage 5: Game Theory... The 'Prisoner's Dilemma' is used to show that:

options.A: Cooperation is the natural state of human interaction.
options.B: Rationality does not always lead to the most mutually beneficial outcome.
options.C: Prisoners are more likely to cooperate than ordinary citizens.
options.D: Mathematics cannot accurately predict human behavior.
options.E: Utility maximization is impossible in a competitive environment.

questions.question: Passage 5: Game Theory... Why do behavioral economists critique traditional Game Theory?

options.A: Because it relies too heavily on biological models.
options.B: Because it assumes humans always act with perfect rationality.
options.C: Because the 'Prisoner's Dilemma' is too simplistic for modern economics.
options.D: Because it was developed by mathematicians rather than social scientists.
options.E: Because it ignores the importance of maximizing personal utility.

questions.question: Passage 5: Game Theory... According to the passage, the scope of Game Theory:

options.A: Has remained strictly within the field of mathematics.
options.B: Has expanded to include various disciplines beyond economics.
options.C: Is limited by its inability to analyze strategic interactions.
options.D: Was originally intended for biological research.
options.E: Is solely focused on resolving the Prisoner's Dilemma.

questions.question: Professor: I've noticed a decline in the quality of your citations in this draft. Student: I apologize; I struggled to find primary sources for the late Ottoman period. Professor: ---- Student: I see. So, focusing on the translated diaries of diplomats would be more acceptable?

options.A: You should try using Wikipedia for a general overview first.
options.B: Primary sources are not really necessary for this specific assignment.
options.C: Perhaps you could look into archives of foreign correspondence from that era.
options.D: You need to finish the bibliography before the end of the week.
options.E: The library is closed for renovations, which might be the problem.

questions.question: Recruiter: We are looking for someone who can manage cross-functional teams under tight deadlines. Candidate: I have five years of experience doing exactly that in the tech sector. Recruiter: ---- Candidate: Certainly. Last year, I led a team of developers and marketers to launch a product two weeks ahead of schedule despite a 20% budget cut.

options.A: Can you give me a specific example of a challenge you faced?
options.B: What is your expected salary for such a demanding role?
options.C: Do you prefer working alone or in a large group?
options.D: We have many candidates with similar experience, why you?
options.E: How do you handle conflict between team members?

questions.question: Librarian: May I help you find something? Patron: Yes, I'm looking for the latest issue of the Journal of Molecular Biology. Librarian: ---- Patron: Oh, I didn't realize. Is there a way to access the digital version from home?

options.A: It is currently being bound and will be unavailable for two weeks.
options.B: We no longer carry physical copies of that journal; it's digital only now.
options.C: You can find it in the periodicals section on the third floor.
options.D: That journal has been discontinued since last year.
options.E: I believe another student has just checked it out.

questions.question: Architect: The client wants to incorporate more sustainable materials into the building's facade. Engineer: That might increase the total weight beyond the current structural limits. Architect: ---- Engineer: Possibly, but we'd need to run a new set of simulations to be sure.

options.A: What if we use lightweight recycled polymers instead of stone?
options.B: The client is not concerned about the budget at this stage.
options.C: Should we tell them it's impossible to meet their request?
options.D: The building is already designed to be earthquake-resistant.
options.E: Why don't we just add more steel support to the foundation?

questions.question: Doctor: Your blood tests show a significant vitamin D deficiency. Patient: That's surprising. I spend quite a bit of time outdoors. Doctor: ---- Patient: That makes sense. I'll start the supplements immediately.

options.A: You should probably stay indoors more to avoid skin damage.
options.B: During winter at this latitude, the sun isn't strong enough for synthesis.
options.C: Vitamin D is primarily found in leafy green vegetables.
options.D: It's likely due to your caffeine consumption in the mornings.
options.E: I will prescribe a stronger antibiotic for your condition.

questions.question: Rarely do we find a scientific discovery that completely invalidates previous theories without offering a more comprehensive framework in their place.

options.A: Most scientific discoveries tend to support existing theories rather than challenging them with new frameworks.
options.B: It is unusual for a discovery to debunk old theories unless it provides a better explanation for the phenomena.
options.C: New theories are often rejected by the scientific community because they fail to account for previous discoveries.
options.D: Scientific progress is slow because new theories rarely provide a comprehensive enough framework.
options.E: Every new discovery eventually invalidates an old theory, creating a more complex understanding of science.

questions.question: Despite the government's efforts to curb inflation through interest rate hikes, the purchasing power of the average citizen continues to dwindle.

options.A: The government has failed to raise interest rates, which is why inflation is affecting people's purchasing power.
options.B: Inflation is decreasing thanks to the government's interest rate policy, though citizens haven't noticed yet.
options.C: Average citizens are losing their buying power even though the government is using higher interest rates to control inflation.
options.D: If the government hadn't raised interest rates, the average citizen's purchasing power would have decreased even faster.
options.E: The dwindling purchasing power of citizens is the main reason the government decided to hike interest rates.

questions.question: Had the warnings about the impending storm been taken more seriously, the scale of the disaster could have been significantly mitigated.

options.A: The disaster was severe because people ignored the serious warnings about the coming storm.
options.B: Even if the warnings had been serious, the scale of the disaster would not have changed.
options.C: The storm was so powerful that no amount of preparation could have reduced the resulting disaster.
options.D: Because the warnings were taken seriously, the disaster was not as bad as it could have been.
options.E: If people had paid more attention to the storm alerts, the damage would have been much less severe.

questions.question: The assumption that technological progress is inherently beneficial to social cohesion is increasingly being questioned by sociologists.

options.A: Sociologists used to believe technology destroyed social cohesion, but they are now changing their minds.
options.B: More and more sociologists are doubting the idea that technology naturally improves the way society holds together.
options.C: Technological progress is known to harm social cohesion, according to most modern sociologists.
options.D: Sociologists are trying to find ways to make technological progress more beneficial for social unity.
options.E: The link between social cohesion and technology is the most important topic for modern sociologists.

questions.question: The concept of 'soft power' was coined by Joseph Nye to describe the ability of a country to influence others through attraction rather than coercion. Unlike hard power, which relies on military force or economic sanctions, soft power stems from a nation's culture, political values, and foreign policies. ----. For instance, the global popularity of American cinema or Japanese animation can enhance the international standing of those countries without a single shot being fired.

options.A: However, hard power remains the most effective tool in modern diplomacy.
options.B: Economic strength is often considered a form of soft power in certain contexts.
options.C: When these elements are perceived as legitimate and moral, they become persuasive tools.
options.D: Many nations are now investing more in their military budgets than in cultural exchange.
options.E: Soft power is often criticized for being too difficult to measure with statistical data.

questions.question: In the realm of architecture, 'form follows function' is a principle associated with late 19th and early 20th-century manufacturing and industrial design. It suggests that the shape of a building or object should primarily relate to its intended purpose. ----. This led to a shift away from the ornate decorations of the Victorian era toward the sleek, minimalist lines of Modernism.

options.A: Architects today often ignore this rule in favor of pure aesthetic expression.
options.B: Ornamentation was seen as a distraction from the structural integrity of the design.
options.C: The principle was first articulated by Louis Sullivan in his essays on skyscrapers.
options.D: Modernist buildings are often criticized for being cold and impersonal to live in.
options.E: Victorian architecture was actually more functional than people realize today.

questions.question: Biotechnology has revolutionized the way we approach rare genetic disorders. By utilizing tools like CRISPR-Cas9, scientists can now 'edit' DNA sequences with unprecedented precision. ----. While the potential to cure lifelong diseases is immense, the possibility of 'designer babies' raises profound moral dilemmas about the future of human evolution.

options.A: This technology is still in its infancy and has not yet been tested on humans.
options.B: However, these advancements bring with them significant ethical concerns.
options.C: DNA sequencing has become much cheaper over the last decade.
options.D: Most genetic disorders are caused by a combination of several different genes.
options.E: The government has recently increased funding for biotechnological research.

questions.question: The Industrial Revolution was not merely a period of technological innovation; it was a fundamental transformation of society. As people moved from rural areas to burgeoning cities, the traditional family structure began to change. ----. This shift laid the groundwork for the modern labor movements and the demand for public education.

options.A: Urban living conditions were often cramped and unsanitary during this time.
options.B: The rise of factories meant that even children were often brought into the workforce.
options.C: Agriculture remained the primary source of income for most European countries.
options.D: Steam power was the most important invention of the 18th century.
options.E: Many people chose to return to their farms after experiencing city life.

questions.question: (I) The Renaissance was a period of intense artistic and intellectual activity that began in Italy. (II) It was marked by a renewed interest in the classical philosophy and literature of Greece and Rome. (III) Many artists of this period were funded by wealthy patrons like the Medici family. (IV) The Black Death had previously wiped out nearly a third of Europe's population in the 14th century. (V) This cultural flowering eventually spread across Europe, influencing everything from science to politics.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Quantum entanglement is a physical phenomenon where pairs of particles interact in such a way that the state of each particle cannot be described independently. (II) This happens even when the particles are separated by large distances. (III) Albert Einstein famously referred to this as 'spooky action at a distance.' (IV) General relativity, on the other hand, describes the gravitational force between massive objects. (V) Recent experiments have confirmed that entanglement is a real and fundamental aspect of quantum mechanics.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Urban heat islands occur when cities replace natural land cover with dense concentrations of pavement and buildings. (II) These surfaces absorb and retain heat, leading to higher temperatures in urban areas compared to their rural surroundings. (III) This effect can be mitigated by planting more trees and installing 'cool roofs.' (IV) Air conditioning was invented by Willis Carrier in 1902 to control humidity in a printing plant. (V) Reducing the heat island effect is crucial for improving the energy efficiency of city buildings.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Language acquisition in children is a complex process that involves both innate biological factors and environmental input. (II) Noam Chomsky proposed the idea of a Universal Grammar to explain how children learn languages so quickly. (III) Many adults find it much more difficult to achieve native-like fluency in a second language. (IV) Behaviorists, however, argued that language is learned primarily through imitation and reinforcement. (V) Despite these differing theories, it is clear that the first few years of life are a critical period for linguistic development.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Sustainable agriculture aims to meet society's food needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs. (II) It focuses on soil health, water conservation, and reducing the use of synthetic pesticides. (III) Organic food sales have seen a steady increase in supermarkets over the last decade. (IV) Crop rotation is a key technique used to maintain soil fertility and break pest cycles. (V) By integrating these practices, farmers can create a more resilient food system.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V
